food_subgroup: Wheat-based soup; broth soup with dough drops

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chinese dough drop soup (Geda Tang). The closest defensible parent is the generic soup food product class FOODON_03311737.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible parent category. No specific code exists for Geda Tang in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chinese dough drop soup or sufficiently close analogue in the clinical terminology system.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chinese dough drop soup weighing approximately 400 g, comprising broth, dough drops, tomato, and egg.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodle soup, Chinese-style, with vegetables (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with USDA SR Legacy entries for wheat flour dough and tomato-egg broth components
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Geda Tang (Chinese dough drop soup). Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis: wheat flour dough drops (~40% of dish weight), tomato-based broth (~45%), egg (~10%), and seasoning (~5%). Reference entries used: USDA FDC #169737 (wheat flour, all-purpose), #170457 (tomatoes, raw), #748967 (egg, whole, raw), and #171287 (chicken broth, homemade-style) as broth proxy. Values represent a diluted, broth-dominant dish consistent with the described preparation. All values are per 100 g of the ready-to-eat soup.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat-flour noodle soup and pasta-in-broth analogues; Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Geda Tang. GI of 52 is estimated from the closest analogous food: wheat-flour noodle soup / pasta in broth (GI ~52, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables and the University of Sydney GI Database. The dough drops are made from plain wheat flour with a high water content and are consumed in a dilute tomato broth, which is consistent with the GI profile of boiled wheat-flour pasta in soup. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 40.8) / 100 ≈ 21.2 g, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 10.2 × 4 = 40.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~280 mg per 100 g; ~1120 mg per 400 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g bowl of Chinese dough drop soup provides approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~21 g per 400 g bowl) from refined wheat flour dough drops
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined wheat flour dough drops contribute a moderate glycemic load of approximately 21 g per standard bowl; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing portion size to 200–250 g, pairing with non-starchy vegetables, or requesting whole-wheat dough drops to attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content (~0.3 g per 100 g) and tomato-based broth providing lycopene
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chinese dough drop soup is relatively low in saturated fat and total fat, and the tomato-based broth provides lycopene, a carotenoid associated with cardiovascular protection; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are also managing blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Dough Drop Soup, known in Mandarin as 疙瘩汤 (Gēda Tāng), is a traditional Northern Chinese home-style soup that has been a staple comfort food for generations. The dish is made by drizzling or pinching small irregular lumps of wheat flour dough — the 'geda' or dough drops — directly into a simmering broth, typically made from tomatoes, egg, and light seasoning such as salt, scallion, and a few drops of sesame oil. The dough drops cook quickly in the hot broth, becoming soft, slightly chewy, and tender, absorbing the savoury tomato flavour. The resulting soup is light yet satisfying, with a mild acidity from the tomato, richness from the egg, and a comforting starchy body from the wheat dough. Nutritionally, the soup is moderate in energy (~62 kcal per 100 g), low in fat (~1.2 g per 100 g), and provides a moderate amount of carbohydrate (~10.2 g per 100 g) primarily from the wheat flour dough drops. The sodium content is notable (~280 mg per 100 g) due to the seasoned broth, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low category), consistent with boiled wheat-flour pasta in broth, making it a moderate glycemic load food per standard serving. The dish contains gluten and egg and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or egg allergy. It is suitable for vegetarians and is generally considered halal. In Singapore, this dish is served at Chinese restaurants specialising in Northern Chinese cuisine and is valued for its simplicity, warmth, and nostalgic appeal.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Geda Tang (Chinese dough drop soup) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were estimated by component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for wheat flour, tomato, egg, and broth. GI was estimated from the closest analogous food (wheat-flour noodle soup in broth) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries (wheat flour, tomato, egg, broth) as no direct database entry for Geda Tang exists in any approved primary regional source. Values should be validated against a laboratory analysis or a future HPB or FAO/INFOODS entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero by formulation as the dish uses no hydrogenated fats. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour noodle soup in broth from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Geda Tang was found in the approved GI literature. 5. The restaurant name '姥姥家疙瘩汤' (Grandma's Geda Tang) from the field survey source is preserved as an alias; it reflects a branded restaurant name rather than the canonical dish name. 6. Sodium content may vary significantly depending on restaurant preparation; the value of 280 mg per 100 g is a mid-range estimate for a lightly seasoned home-style preparation. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA component data and are approximate. 8. All -1 sentinel values represent nutrients not reported or not reliably estimable from the component sources used.
